title: Separate reversible withdrawal from terminal retirement in policy lifecycle
context: Policy lifecycle had a documented gap (§57): no way to return advisory authority to humans mid-stream or definitively close a rule while preserving history. Demote existed (blocking→advisory) but withdrawal (advisory→proposed) was missing, breaking the authority loop.
decision: Implement two distinct terminal operations: withdrawPolicy (active_advisory→proposed, reversible, re-enters escalation loop) and retirePolicy (active/proposed→retired, terminal, history immutable). Both require explicit human actor and audited reason; neither erases history.
consequences: Withdrawal is reversible — policy re-enters escalation loop without losing audit trail, enabling re-evaluation
Retirement is terminal and irreversible — once retired, policy stops surfacing but full history/audit remain (HC-08 supersede-never-erase)
Audit trail distinguishes 'withdrawn' (scope/premise changed) from 'retired' (rule definitively done)
Authority explicitly returns to human pool on withdrawal, making escalation loop semantics clear
Proposed policies can now be directly retired without flowing through advisory first
alternatives_rejected: Single 'deactivate' operation — conflates re-evaluation with finality, loses reversibility semantics
Withdrawal as terminal — would prevent escalation loop from re-asking the question
Retirement erasing history — violates supersede-never-erase principle and audit accountability

rejected_tripwires.alternative: Single 'deactivate' operation — conflates re-evaluation with finality, loses reversibility semantics
Withdrawal as terminal — would prevent escalation loop from re-asking the question
Retirement erasing history — violates supersede-never-erase principle and audit accountability
